title: We are creating Asia-capable Australians. But are we keeping them?
gist: Australia measures enrolment in Asia-capability programs but has no longitudinal data on whether it retains and uses the graduates it produces — with anecdotal evidence that language-skilled Australians stay abroad because their skills are more valued overseas than at home.

insights.claim: Australia counts how many people enrol in Asia programs but has no longitudinal data on where Asia-capable graduates go, whether they return, or whether their skills are used domestically.
insights.speaker: Andre Kwok
insights.so_what: The measured metric (enrolment) misses the outcome that matters (retention/utilisation) — a policy blind spot.
insights.locator: Australia has limited longitudinal data on the pathways

insights.claim: The unanswered question in Australia's Asia-capability agenda is retention and utilisation of skills, not the volume of the training pipeline.
insights.speaker: Andre Kwok
insights.so_what: Reframes a decade of policy from producing capability to keeping and deploying it.

insights.claim: Australians with strong Mandarin, Japanese and Bahasa Indonesian proficiency often choose to stay or return overseas, believing language and intercultural skills are more readily recognised and rewarded abroad than in Australia.
insights.speaker: Andre Kwok
insights.so_what: Anecdotal talent leakage suggests Australia may be subsidising human capital that leaves.
insights.locator: While living overseas, I met Australians

insights.claim: A real and perceived mismatch exists between the narrative that Asia capability matters and the limited workforce recognition of those skills in Australia, where they are treated as an optional CV nice-to-have.
insights.speaker: Andre Kwok
insights.so_what: Weak employer demand undercuts the policy push — the retention problem is demand-side, not supply-side.
insights.locator: There also needs to be clearer messaging

insights.claim: Governments and education institutions should track Asia-capability alumni cohorts at three, five and ten years post-program to build a feedback loop on policy impact.
insights.speaker: Andre Kwok
insights.so_what: A concrete measurement fix that would reveal whether Asia-capability spending pays off.
insights.locator: Governments and education institutions should explore

insights.claim: Existing tools such as DFAT's Australia Awards alumni tracer (last updated 2023) and ACICIS alumni research are partial, leaving Australia without a comprehensive picture of its Asia-capable alumni population.
insights.speaker: Andre Kwok
insights.so_what: The evidence base is thin and dated — a data gap before any confident policy claim.
insights.locator: While DFAT's Australia Awards alumni tracer

top_entities: Australia in the Asian Century White Paper
